items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.
Correct answer: 1896. “1776” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: 1863. “1840” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year of the first modern Olympic Games?
items.why: Exact answer: 1896. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
